summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'particle' in the context of the mole?

You must specify what the particle is: 1 mole of NaCl = 6.022 × 10²³ formula units, while 1 mole of H₂O = 6.022 × 10²³ molecules.

What is the relationship between moles, mass, and Avogadro's number?

The mole sits at the center of the conversion map: grams ÷ molar mass → moles → × Avogadro's number → particles.

A student says '1 mole of NaCl contains 6.022 × 10²³ atoms.' What is the error?

NaCl is ionic — it doesn't form molecules or isolated atoms. One mole of NaCl = 6.022 × 10²³ formula units, each containing one Na⁺ and one Cl⁻ ion.

A student converts 1.806 × 10²⁴ molecules of CO₂ to moles. What is the answer?

1.806 × 10²⁴ ÷ 6.022 × 10²³ = 3.00 mol. The student divides the particle count by Avogadro's number.
